aggregationRule: Apply each component rubric to the combined evidence for the four selected jurisdictions as a holistic ordinal judgment. Do not calculate or average jurisdiction sub-scores. Use the highest anchor whose core conditions are supported; retain a null score when the evidence cannot distinguish adjacent anchors.
globalEvidenceRule: Evidence marked Global may provide context for the field but cannot by itself establish that the same condition holds in each selected jurisdiction.

anchors.0: Public institutions routinely direct frontier-model training, release, deployment, and access decisions across the selected jurisdictions.
anchors.1: Public operational control is broad across the selected jurisdictions and non-public provider discretion is limited to narrow implementation choices.
anchors.2: Control is mixed across jurisdictions or lifecycle stages, with meaningful public direction alongside substantial provider discretion.
anchors.3: Providers make most training, release, deployment, and access decisions; public intervention is episodic, constraining, or post hoc rather than operational.
anchors.4: Near-exclusive non-public control of training, release, deployment, and access is demonstrated across the full selected scope, with no routine public ability to direct lifecycle decisions.

anchors.0: Public institutions control or have guaranteed access to the compute, chips, model weights, training data, and infrastructure required for frontier-model development across the selected jurisdictions.
anchors.1: Broad public provision or mandated access substantially offsets dependence on non-public critical resources across the selected jurisdictions.
anchors.2: Resource control is mixed, with viable public or diversified alternatives alongside material non-public bottlenecks.
anchors.3: Most frontier-relevant compute, weights, training assets, or infrastructure are non-public and viable public alternatives are limited.
anchors.4: A narrow non-public group controls indispensable frontier-model resources across the selected scope and no viable public substitute or guaranteed access exists.

anchors.0: Public institutions receive complete, timely, and verifiable information about training, testing, capabilities, safeguards, incidents, and deployment across the selected jurisdictions.
anchors.1: Broad mandatory technical access exists across the selected jurisdictions, with only minor timing or detail gaps.
anchors.2: Meaningful mandatory access exists in some jurisdictions or information categories, but important lifecycle or coverage gaps remain.
anchors.3: Public institutions depend heavily on selective provider disclosure and technical-information obligations are fragmented across jurisdictions or model categories.
anchors.4: Essential technical and operational information is inaccessible to public institutions across the selected scope and no compulsory access route exists.

anchors.0: Public institutions can independently test complete frontier systems through compulsory, timely access to relevant model variants, safeguards, tools, and technical information.
anchors.1: Independent public evaluation access is broad across the selected jurisdictions, with only occasional dependence on provider assistance.
anchors.2: Substantial public evaluation capacity exists, but model access, system coverage, timing, or access to internals is inconsistent.
anchors.3: Public evaluation depends mainly on provider-granted access, configurations, technical assistance, or information, subject to limited statutory exceptions.
anchors.4: Public institutions cannot independently evaluate frontier systems and must rely wholly on provider claims, tools, and discretionary access.

anchors.0: Public frontier-model expertise equals or exceeds non-public capacity across the selected jurisdictions and covers all required technical functions.
anchors.1: Durable public teams cover nearly all frontier-governance needs, with only minor hiring, retention, or specialization gaps.
anchors.2: Meaningful public expertise exists, but material hiring, retention, scale, or specialized-capability gaps persist.
anchors.3: Leading expertise is concentrated in frontier laboratories and contractors, while public capacity is limited or uneven across the selected jurisdictions.
anchors.4: Essential frontier-model expertise is almost entirely outside public institutions across the selected scope, creating near-total dependence on non-public actors.

anchors.0: No selected jurisdiction has a binding mandate directed at general-purpose or frontier-model governance.
anchors.1: Authority rests mainly on general law, voluntary commitments, guidance, or ad hoc arrangements rather than binding frontier-model duties.
anchors.2: Binding duties exist in limited jurisdictions, for limited model categories, or for limited lifecycle stages.
anchors.3: Binding frontier-model duties cover most of the selected field, but material jurisdictional gaps, exemptions, or implementation fragmentation remain.
anchors.4: Comprehensive and operational binding mandates cover general-purpose and frontier models throughout their lifecycle in all four selected jurisdictions.

anchors.0: Public institutions have no power to compel frontier-model technical, training, evaluation, incident, or deployment information.
anchors.1: Compulsory access is narrow, ad hoc, or limited to post-incident investigation under general powers.
anchors.2: Compulsory access exists in limited jurisdictions or information categories, with major lifecycle or territorial gaps.
anchors.3: Broad compulsory powers operate across most of the selected field, but material scope, timing, or coordination gaps remain.
anchors.4: Public institutions have timely, comprehensive, and enforceable access to relevant frontier-model information across all selected jurisdictions.

anchors.0: Public institutions have neither legal power nor technical capacity to evaluate frontier models independently.
anchors.1: Public technical capacity or voluntary testing arrangements exist, but institutions cannot compel access or evaluation.
anchors.2: Legal evaluation power or capable public institutions exist in part of the selected scope, with substantial access or coverage limitations.
anchors.3: Broad independent evaluation power covers most of the selected field, but jurisdictional, lifecycle, or technical fragmentation remains.
anchors.4: Routine, compulsory, full-access independent evaluation is legally and operationally available throughout the model lifecycle in all selected jurisdictions.

anchors.0: Public institutions have no power to require frontier-model corrections or impose sanctions.
anchors.1: Institutions rely on indirect general-law remedies, advice, or voluntary requests rather than dedicated binding powers.
anchors.2: Binding corrective or sanctioning powers exist in limited jurisdictions or scope, or are not yet fully operational.
anchors.3: Substantial mitigation, withdrawal, recall, suspension, or penalty powers cover most of the selected field, with residual fragmentation.
anchors.4: Comprehensive and operational corrective and enforcement powers cover all relevant lifecycle stages and all four selected jurisdictions.

anchors.0: No selected jurisdiction has defined institutional coverage or coordination for general-purpose or frontier-model governance.
anchors.1: Coverage consists mainly of isolated initiatives, advisory bodies, or voluntary forums without defined operational roles.
anchors.2: Relevant institutions exist across parts of the selected scope, but material gaps, overlaps, or non-binding coordination remain.
anchors.3: Formal coverage and coordination span most jurisdictions and lifecycle stages, with residual fragmentation or unclear responsibility.
anchors.4: Clear, operational lifecycle and cross-jurisdiction coordination covers the full selected field with defined responsibilities and effective information sharing.
